div.div: One player picks up a pair of scissors and passes them to the next person, announcing 'I pass these crossed' or 'I pass these not crossed.' New players assume this refers to whether the scissors are open or closed — they're wrong. The actual rule: 'crossed' means the passer's legs are crossed at the ankles, 'not crossed' means they're not. The scissors' position is a red herring. Those who already know play along perfectly; newcomers slowly figure out that they're not looking at the right thing. The moment of realization is the payoff.

Scissors Crossed or Not Crossed is a classic catch-game that exploits the principle of misdirection — everyone looks at the scissors when the key information is actually elsewhere. Victorian parlor books called these 'mystification games.'

A pair of scissors is passed around the group. Each time, the passer announces whether they pass them 'crossed' or 'not crossed.' New players cannot understand the pattern — the real secret is not the scissors' position but whether the passer's legs (ankles) are crossed or uncrossed when they pass.

svg.text: Source: The Sociable, or One Thousand and One Home Amusements (1858) · Heritage Parlor
